lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Where wilt thou lead me? speak; I’ll go no further.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Mark me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: My hour is almost come,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: When I to sulphurous and tormenting flames

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Must render up myself.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Alas, poor ghost!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To what I shall unfold.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Speak; I am bound to hear.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What?

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: I am thy father’s spirit,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Doom’d for a certain term to walk the night,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And for the day confined to fast in fires,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Till the foul crimes done in my days of nature

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Are burnt and purged away. But that I am forbid

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To tell the secrets of my prison-house,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: I could a tale unfold whose lightest word

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Would harrow up thy soul, freeze thy young blood,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Make thy two eyes, like stars, start from their spheres,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Thy knotted and combined locks to part

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And each particular hair to stand on end,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Like quills upon the fretful porpentine:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But this eternal blazon must not be

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To ears of flesh and blood. List, list, O, list!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: If thou didst ever thy dear father love—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O God!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Revenge his foul and most unnatural murder.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Murder!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Murder most foul, as in the best it is;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But this most foul, strange and unnatural.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Haste me to know’t, that I, with wings as swift

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As meditation or the thoughts of love,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: May sweep to my revenge.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: I find thee apt;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And duller shouldst thou be than the fat weed

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: That roots itself in ease on Lethe wharf,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Wouldst thou not stir in this. Now, Hamlet, hear:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: ’Tis given out that, sleeping in my orchard,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: A serpent stung me; so the whole ear of Denmark

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Is by a forged process of my death

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Rankly abused: but know, thou noble youth,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The serpent that did sting thy father’s life

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Now wears his crown.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O my prophetic soul!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My uncle!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Ay, that incestuous, that adulterate beast,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: With witchcraft of his wit, with traitorous gifts—

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: O wicked wit and gifts, that have the power

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: So to seduce!—won to his shameful lust

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The will of my most seeming-virtuous queen:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: O Hamlet, what a falling-off was there!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: From me, whose love was of that dignity

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: That it went hand in hand even with the vow

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: I made to her in marriage, and to decline

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Upon a wretch whose natural gifts were poor

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To those of mine!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But virtue, as it never will be moved,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Though lewdness court it in a shape of heaven,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: So lust, though to a radiant angel link’d,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Will sate itself in a celestial bed,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And prey on garbage.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But, soft! methinks I scent the morning air;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Brief let me be. Sleeping within my orchard,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: My custom always of the afternoon,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Upon my secure hour thy uncle stole,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: With juice of cursed hebenon in a vial,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And in the porches of my ears did pour

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The leperous distilment; whose effect

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Holds such an enmity with blood of man

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: That swift as quicksilver it courses through

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The natural gates and alleys of the body,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And with a sudden vigour doth posset

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And curd, like eager droppings into milk,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The thin and wholesome blood: so did it mine;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And a most instant tetter bark’d about,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: All my smooth body.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Thus was I, sleeping, by a brother’s hand

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Of life, of crown, of queen, at once dispatch’d:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Cut off even in the blossoms of my sin,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Unhousel’d, disappointed, unaneled,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: No reckoning made, but sent to my account

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: With all my imperfections on my head:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: O, horrible! O, horrible! most horrible!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: If thou hast nature in thee, bear it not;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Let not the royal bed of Denmark be

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: A couch for luxury and damned incest.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But, howsoever thou pursuest this act,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Taint not thy mind, nor let thy soul contrive

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Against thy mother aught: leave her to heaven

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And to those thorns that in her bosom lodge,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To prick and sting her. Fare thee well at once!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The glow-worm shows the matin to be near,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And ’gins to pale his uneffectual fire:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Adieu, adieu! Hamlet, remember me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O all you host of heaven! O earth! what else?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And shall I couple hell? O, fie! Hold, hold, my heart;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And you, my sinews, grow not instant old,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But bear me stiffly up. Remember thee!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, thou poor ghost, while memory holds a seat

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In this distracted globe. Remember thee!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Yea, from the table of my memory

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll wipe away all trivial fond records,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: All saws of books, all forms, all pressures past,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That youth and observation copied there;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And thy commandment all alone shall live

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Within the book and volume of my brain,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Unmix’d with baser matter: yes, by heaven!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O most pernicious woman!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O villain, villain, smiling, damned villain!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My tables—meet it is I set it down,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That one may smile, and smile, and be a villain;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: At least I’m sure it may be so in Denmark: Writing.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So, uncle, there you are. Now to my word;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It is “Adieu, adieu! remember me.”

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I have sworn’t.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, my lord—

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Lord Hamlet—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Heaven secure him!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So be it!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Hillo, ho, ho, my lord!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hillo, ho, ho, boy! come, bird, come.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: How is’t, my noble lord?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What news, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, wonderful!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Good my lord, tell it.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No; you’ll reveal it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Not I, my lord, by heaven.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Nor I, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How say you, then; would heart of man once think it?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But you’ll be secret?

lines.speaker: HORATIO MARCELLUS
lines.text: Ay, by heaven, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: There’s ne’er a villain dwelling in all Denmark

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But he’s an arrant knave.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: There needs no ghost, my lord, come from the grave

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: To tell us this.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, right; you are i’ the right;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And so, without more circumstance at all,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I hold it fit that we shake hands and part:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You, as your business and desire shall point you;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For every man has business and desire,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Such as it is; and for mine own poor part,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Look you, I’ll go pray.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: These are but wild and whirling words, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’m sorry they offend you, heartily;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Yes, ’faith heartily.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: There’s no offence, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Yes, by Saint Patrick, but there is, Horatio,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And much offence too. Touching this vision here,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It is an honest ghost, that let me tell you:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For your desire to know what is between us,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O’ermaster’t as you may. And now, good friends,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As you are friends, scholars and soldiers,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Give me one poor request.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What is’t, my lord? we will.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Never make known what you have seen to-night.

lines.speaker: HORATIO MARCELLUS
lines.text: My lord, we will not.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nay, but swear’t.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In faith,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: My lord, not I.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Nor I, my lord, in faith.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Upon my sword.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: We have sworn, my lord, already.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Indeed, upon my sword, indeed.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Beneath. Swear.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ah, ha, boy! say’st thou so? art thou there, truepenny?

lines.text: Beneath. Swear.

lines.text: Hic et ubique? then we’ll shift our ground.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Come hither, gentlemen,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And lay your hands again upon my sword:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Never to speak of this that you have heard,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Swear by my sword.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Beneath. Swear.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Well said, old mole! canst work i’ the earth so fast?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A worthy pioner! Once more remove, good friends.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: O day and night, but this is wondrous strange!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And therefore as a stranger give it welcome.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Than are dreamt of in your philosophy.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But come;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Here, as before, never, so help you mercy,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How strange or odd soe’er I bear myself,

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To put an antic disposition on,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That you, at such times seeing me, never shall,

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or by pronouncing of some doubtful phrase,

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That you know aught of me: this not to do,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So grace and mercy at your most need help you,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Swear.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Beneath. Swear.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Rest, rest, perturbed spirit! They swear. So, gentlemen,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With all my love I do commend me to you:

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And still your fingers on your lips, I pray.

lines.speaker: HAMLET

glosses.definition: the time I must return (to purgatory) approaches
obliged to listen (with a pun on 'ready to run')

glosses.phrase: foul crimes done in my days of nature

glosses.definition: break up and agitate your soul
leap out of their orbits (eye sockets)
bank of the river Lethe (river of forgetfulness in the underworld)
feed on refuse (Gertrude descending to Claudius)
poison from the hebenon plant (henbane or yew)

glosses.phrase: gates and alleys of the body
glosses.definition: passages and channels of the body
weak light (of the glow-worm, paling at dawn)

glosses.phrase: meet it is I set it down
glosses.definition: it is fitting I record it
truthful ghost (not a devil in disguise)

glosses.phrase: Never make known what you have seen

glosses.definition: underground chambers (as if the ghost is below the stage)

glosses.phrase: more things in heaven and earth
glosses.definition: more realities than your philosophy imagines

annotations.title: The ghost's purgatorial fires
annotations.body: The ghost's self-description — 'confined to fast in fires' until 'the foul crimes done in my days of nature / Are burnt and purged away' — places him unambiguously in purgatory, a doctrine the Church of England had officially abolished in 1547. Stephen Greenblatt argues that Hamlet is structured around this confessional impossibility: a son at a Protestant university receives a commission from a father whose very existence depends on a theology his education has taught him to reject. The ghost cannot reveal 'the secrets of my prison-house'; if he could, the fires would stop making sense within the play's official Protestant frame. Shakespeare keeps the ghost's ontology just fuzzy enough to allow both Catholic and Protestant audiences their own reading.

annotations.title: The murder narrated in real time
annotations.body: The ghost does not merely report the murder; he re-enacts it. 'Sleeping within my orchard' establishes the scene; 'the juice of cursed hebenon' is poured; the poison's progress is tracked through blood, skin, and 'natural gates and alleys of the body.' The narrative takes its time — some forty lines — so that the audience experiences the murder in something close to its own duration. The technique is a Shakespearean innovation: the ghost makes his son an eyewitness to a crime he did not see. The scene Hamlet will later stage for Claudius in 3.2 is this same murder performed again, now by actors before its architect. Revenge in Hamlet is always a rehearsal of the original crime.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: 'Taint not thy mind' — the ghost's ethical commission
annotations.body: The ghost's final instruction complicates the commission of revenge: 'But howsoever thou pursuest this act, / Taint not thy mind, nor let thy soul contrive / Against thy mother aught.' The son is to avenge the murder but spare the guilty wife, and he is to do it without corrupting himself. This ethical double-bind will drive Hamlet's behavior for four acts. Recent critics from Margreta de Grazia to Rhodri Lewis have argued that the play is less about delay than about the impossibility of carrying out this commission honorably — a truly revenging son would be a ruined man. 'Taint not thy mind' is the instruction on which the whole plot founders.
sources: Rhodri Lewis, Hamlet and the Vision of Darkness (2017)
Margreta de Grazia, Hamlet without Hamlet (2007)

annotations.title: 'My tables — meet it is I set it down'
annotations.body: Hamlet's note-taking after the ghost's departure is the scene's strangest moment. He is a man who has just been commissioned by his murdered father's spirit, and his immediate response is scholarly: he reaches for his writing-tablet to record the observation that 'one may smile, and smile, and be a villain.' Elizabethan tables were small erasable booklets of plaster-coated pages, used by students and lawyers; Hamlet's reach for his is a signal that his training at Wittenberg still governs him. The dissonance is precisely what Shakespeare exploits — in the moment of purest emotion, his hero behaves like a graduate student. This comic undertone recurs throughout the play and prevents its tragedy from curdling into melodrama.

annotations.title: 'The time is out of joint' — the tragedy of restoration
annotations.body: Hamlet's closing couplet — 'The time is out of joint: O cursed spite, / That ever I was born to set it right!' — names the tragic task with eerie precision. 'Out of joint' is anatomical: a time dislocated from itself. 'Set it right' is reparative: to put a bone back in its socket. The tragedy will consist in Hamlet's discovery that restoration is not available to him; setting the time right will require destroying everyone who occupies it, including himself. The line is also where the play first admits that Hamlet did not choose his role — 'born to set it right' — a determinism the Wittenberg student never reconciles with his Protestant theology of free will.
sources: G. Wilson Knight, The Wheel of Fire (1930)

trials.prompt: According to the ghost, how did he die?
options: A serpent bit him while he slept in his orchard — the official story.
Claudius poured poison into his ear while he slept in his orchard.
He was stabbed in the night by an unknown assailant.
He died of an illness Claudius had secretly encouraged.

trials.prompt: The ghost's description of being 'confined to fast in fires' most precisely places him in which theological location?
options: Hell, the Catholic place of damnation.
Purgatory, the Catholic place of purification — officially abolished in the Church of England.
Limbo, the place of unbaptized souls.
Sheol, the Old Testament place of the dead.

trials.prompt: What specific constraint does the ghost place on how Hamlet should carry out his revenge?
options: He must do it publicly before the whole court.
He must not harm his mother and must not corrupt his own mind in the process.
He must do it within the month or the ghost will return to haunt him.
He must do it without the help of any friends.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet mean by reaching for his 'tables' immediately after the ghost's departure?
options: He is about to write a letter to Ophelia.
He is taking out a small writing-tablet to record his observations — a scholar's instinct in a moment of tragedy.
He is preparing to cast a spell to summon the ghost back.
He is setting up a table for Horatio's interrogation.

trials.prompt: What strategy does Hamlet announce he will adopt for the rest of the play?
options: Open rebellion against the King.
Secret flight to Wittenberg to gather allies.
An 'antic disposition' — a put-on madness — to disguise his intentions.
Waiting silently until the ghost returns with further instructions.

trials.prompt: What does the image 'the time is out of joint' suggest?
options: That winter has come unseasonably early.
That time is dislocated like a bone out of its socket, and Hamlet is called to reset it.
That Hamlet's wristwatch has broken.
That Denmark has abandoned the Christian calendar.
